description:
You are acting as a junior storage-administrator who has just logged in as the normal, unprivileged user “user”.
Your job is to free space that is being wasted by old application logs, archive them, and then leave a **precisely formatted** textual report that management’s automated dashboard will later ingest.

Current situation
-----------------
1. A directory “/home/user/sample_logs” already exists.  
2. Inside it there are exactly three files:
      • /home/user/sample_logs/log1.log  
      • /home/user/sample_logs/log2.log  
      • /home/user/sample_logs/log3.log  
   Every file is *exactly* 1 048 576 bytes (1 MiB) of data; total = 3 147 648 bytes.  
3. No directory called “/home/user/archive” exists yet.

End-goal
--------
When you finish, the system **must** look like this:

A. The directory “/home/user/archive” exists.  
B. A compressed archive named  
      /home/user/archive/logs_20240614.tar.gz  
   contains all three original “.log” files (and nothing else).  
C. The original *.log* files have been deleted from “/home/user/sample_logs” so that that directory is now empty (but the directory itself must remain in place).  
D. A plain-text report file exists here:  
      /home/user/archive/log_archive_report.txt  

Report file – exact required format
-----------------------------------
The report file **must contain exactly three lines**, each terminated by a single newline character “\n”, and in the order shown here:

1. Original files archived: 3  
2. Archive name: logs_20240614.tar.gz  
3. Original total size (bytes): 3145728  

Nothing else (no blank lines, no extra spaces).

Constraints & advice
--------------------
• Use only standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities available in a typical minimal installation (tar, gzip, stat, mv, mkdir, echo, etc.).  
• Make sure to create the “archive” directory before attempting to move or create files inside it.  
• Verify that “/home/user/sample_logs” is empty **after** the archive step; this is what actually frees up disk space.  
• Be careful with paths; the automated checker will look strictly at the absolute paths mentioned above.  
• The names and capitalisation of directories and files are case-sensitive.  
• Do not run anything that requires root (sudo) privileges; everything must be doable as the normal “user”.  
• Feel free to echo informative progress messages to the console, but the *only* file that will be parsed for validation is “/home/user/archive/log_archive_report.txt”.

If any of the four end-state items (A-D) is missing, incorrectly named, or the report format deviates even slightly, the automated test will fail. Perform each step in the terminal in the correct order so that the final system state matches the specification exactly.
truth:
Initial state created by the test harness
-----------------------------------------
1. Directory: /home/user/sample_logs/
2. Files (each exactly 1 048 576 bytes, filled with repeating “A” characters):
      /home/user/sample_logs/log1.log
      /home/user/sample_logs/log2.log
      /home/user/sample_logs/log3.log
3. No directory named /home/user/archive/ yet.

Required final state
--------------------
Directories:
• /home/user/sample_logs/          (exists and is empty)
• /home/user/archive/              (exists)

Files:
1. /home/user/archive/logs_20240614.tar.gz
   – Must be a valid gzip-compressed tar archive.
   – Tar archive must contain precisely:
        ./log1.log
        ./log2.log
        ./log3.log
     (paths stored **without** the leading “/home/user/sample_logs”, i.e. relative paths).
2. /home/user/archive/log_archive_report.txt
   – Exactly 3 lines, UTF-8 encoded text, newline “\n” after each line, with content:
        Original files archived: 3
        Archive name: logs_20240614.tar.gz
        Original total size (bytes): 3145728

Verification logic
------------------
1. stat -c%s /home/user/sample_logs/log*.log should return “cannot stat … No such file” (they’re deleted).
2. test -f /home/user/archive/logs_20240614.tar.gz   → should succeed.
3. tar -tzf /home/user/archive/logs_20240614.tar.gz produces exactly three entries: “log1.log”, “log2.log”, “log3.log”.
4. cat /home/user/archive/log_archive_report.txt must match the three-line template shown above **verbatim**.
name: task_000000_d19b3d40
